title: Why Do Pro-Lifers Despise Abolitionists?
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman, a former pro-life activist turned abolitionist, argues that pro-life leaders reject abolitionism not because abolitionists are rude or divisive, but because abolitionism threatens their political power and demands repentance for decades of incrementalist failure.
audiences: Abolitionist activists wrestling with tone critiques
Christians weighing the call to repentance
Curious onlookers observing the pro-life vs. abolitionist conflict

questions.question: What was the author's initial theory about why pro-life leaders rejected abolitionism, and how was it disproven?
questions.answer: Silberman initially believed pro-life leaders rejected abolitionism because of abolitionists' attitudes, so he set out to be the 'nice abolitionist.' His theory collapsed almost immediately when even a maximally gentle, nuanced critique of Abby Johnson provoked belligerent slander and accusations from a pro-life friend, demonstrating that the issue was not tone but substance.
questions.quote: I decided I was going to be the nice abolitionist. Surely people wouldn’t reject the ideas if they were explained to them kindly and articulately, I thought. My theory was quickly disproven.

questions.question: According to Silberman, what is the real reason pro-life leaders reject abolitionism?
questions.answer: He argues the rejection stems from threats to political power and personal pride, not from any genuine grievance about abolitionist behavior. Pro-life leaders cannot admit that decades of incremental strategy have failed, so they manufacture justifications for refusing immediate abolition.
questions.quote: The pro-life leaders who reject abolitionism do so because the Abolitionist Movement is a threat to the political power of the Pro-Life Establishment and we’re a threat to the pride of people who don’t want to admit that they haven’t been going about the fight against abortion rightly.

questions.question: How does Silberman defend the harsh tone abolitionists sometimes take toward pro-life politicians?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activists wrestling with tone critiques
questions.answer: He concedes abolitionists are harsh, but insists the harshness is a consequence of pro-life refusal to abolish abortion, not the cause of it. The criticisms follow specific failures, such as senators going to the media against SB13, rather than preceding them.
questions.quote: But that harshness is a response to the refusal of these people and groups to immediately abolish abortion, not what causes them to refuse to abolish abortion.

questions.question: Why does Silberman say abolitionist criticism targets the pro-life lobby rather than the pro-abortion lobby?
questions.audience: Curious onlookers observing the pro-life vs. abolitionist conflict
questions.answer: Because in states like Oklahoma the pro-abortion lobby lacks the political power to stop abolition. With pro-life supermajorities holding every relevant office, the actual decision-makers blocking abolition are pro-lifers, so that is where pressure must be applied.
questions.quote: It’s pro-lifers who have the choice before them of whether to abolish abortion. That is where the pressure must be applied.

questions.question: What role does repentance play in Silberman's argument, and why does he frame it as a Christian obligation?
questions.audience: Christians weighing the call to repentance
questions.answer: Silberman cites pastor Brett Baggett to argue that repentance is normal Christian life, not something shameful. Since incrementalism has driven groups like National Right to Life to actively oppose immediate abolition, the foundational ideology is wrong and demands public repentance rather than continued defense.
questions.quote: When we’re wrong, the Christian obligation is to own it. The fact that adherence to pro-life incrementalism has driven organizations like National Right to Life to become the primary opposition to the immediate abolition of abortion is evidence that something is terribly wrong at the foundation of the ideology, and therefore the movement.

questions.question: How does Silberman use William Lloyd Garrison as a model for handling past pro-life involvement?
questions.answer: He highlights the lesser-known passage in The Liberator where Garrison publicly recanted his earlier endorsement of gradual abolition. Silberman explicitly mirrors this act, recanting his own past support for incremental pro-life bills and calling others to do the same.
questions.quote: I, too, recant and repent for my unthinking past endorsement of incremental/gradual pro-life bills. As difficult as it may be for some, I earnestly plead and pray that many more join me in that repentance and join me in demanding total and immediate abolition without compromise.

questions.question: What kinds of slander does Silberman say pro-lifers spread about abolitionists, and how does he respond?
questions.audience: Abolitionist activists wrestling with tone critiques
questions.answer: He recounts being publicly labeled 'evil,' equated with 'God hates fa*s' sign-holders, and accused of calling unwed mothers 'whores.' He flatly denies these characterizations and says anyone who actually behaved that way would not be welcome in the movement.
questions.quote: This is vicious and willful slander. Anyone who refers to unwed mothers as “whores” or holds signs saying “God hates fa*s” is not welcome in the Abolitionist Movement.

questions.question: What pattern does Silberman identify in how Free the States escalated its rhetoric against specific Oklahoma senators?
questions.audience: Curious onlookers observing the pro-life vs. abolitionist conflict
questions.answer: He documents that Free the States only criticized senators like Smalley, Treat, and McCortney after those officials publicly worked against SB13. The group even ran positive flyer campaigns urging McCortney toward heroism before he ultimately killed the bill.
questions.quote: Free the States didn’t make one ill-statement toward Senators Jason Smalley or Greg Treat until they went to the media to spread anti-SB13 talking points.

related.title: Abby Johnson Says She Cares More About Federal Programs Than Abolishing Abortion
related.reason: Silberman's catalyzing dispute centered on linking to this exact piece about Abby Johnson opposing Texas abolition legislation.

related.title: Four Problems With David Barton's Incrementalism Apologia
related.reason: Directly engages the incrementalist ideology Silberman calls on pro-lifers to repent of.

related.title: Incrementalism Versus Immediatism in a Nutshell
related.reason: Provides the foundational contrast between the two strategies that drives the conflict described in this article.

related.title: Senator McCortney Again Denies SB13 a Fair Hearing
related.reason: Concrete documentation of the McCortney/SB13 episode Silberman uses as evidence of pro-life obstruction.

related.title: Idaho Pro-Life State Senator Says Ending Abortion 'Destroys the Pro-Life Movement'
related.reason: Another case study in pro-life politicians publicly opposing immediate abolition, reinforcing Silberman's thesis about institutional self-protection.

related.title: Abolitionist, Not Pro-Life
related.reason: Articulates the categorical distinction between abolitionism and the pro-life movement at the heart of this article's conflict.

related.title: The Three Pro-Life Men Keeping Abortion Legal in Oklahoma
related.reason: Names the specific pro-life Oklahoma officeholders Silberman references when arguing pressure belongs on pro-lifers, not pro-aborts.
